summary::A Network Allow-List Won't Stop: A Network Allow-List Won't Stop Exfiltration is a technical article discussing how network allow-lists can fail to prevent exfiltration, specifically when it comes to DNS lookups and data flowing through authorized channels. This means that even if an application is running within a sandbox with a network policy, it can still exfiltrate sensitive data through authorized channels such as DNS lookups.

summary::What is Git made of? (2022): Git is made up of objects, including blobs, trees, and commits, which are stored in hidden directories such as .git/objects/info/.git/objects/pack, .git/refs/heads, and .git/refs/tags. These objects contain the actual file contents, commit information, and other metadata. summary::Childhood Computing: Childhood Computing By Susam Pal on 24 May 2026 I recently stumbled upon a blog post titled Childhood Computing that made me think about my own childhood computing experience. summary::libwce: the entropy layer of a: libwce is a bare-bones entropy layer implementation that compresses wavelet coefficients by estimating their bit-plane count from neighboring groups, rather than tracking individual pixels like older codecs. This approach allows for more efficient compression of similar color and brightness values in the image, resulting in smaller file sizes while maintaining acceptable quality. By using BPC coding with groups of four coefficients at a time, libwce can compress wavelet coefficients down to a small number of significant bits, making it suitable for applications where low-bitrate video is required.
summary::Constraint Decay: The Fragility: Large Language Model agents demonstrate strong performance in autonomous code generation under loose specifications, but production-grade software requires strict adherence to structural constraints such as architectural patterns databases and object-relational mappings. summary::FreeBSD Foundation Executive Di: Deb Goodkin, Executive Director of the FreeBSD Foundation, has been trying daily to run FreeBSD on her laptop desktop hardware for over a year, despite previous experiences with similar efforts being met with frustration and time-consuming results. She used a Framework Laptop to test FreeBSD's performance and functionality, but encountered issues such as Zoom failing video calls and Microsoft Teams only partially working. With the help of online resources, Goodkin was able to successfully run FreeBSD daily on her laptop.
summary::Memory has grown to nearly two-: High-bandwidth memory has grown to nearly two-thirds of total AI chip component costs, with estimates suggesting it will account for an even larger share in 2026 as memory supply remains tight and prices rise. This shift is driven by the increasing demand for high-performance computing and artificial intelligence applications that require fast data transfer rates. As a result, companies such as Nvidia, AMD, Google, and Amazon are investing heavily in developing new technologies to support these growing demands.
summary::Flick (YC F25) Is Hiring Front: Flick, a company that aims to redefine the interface for AI native filmmaking, is hiring a senior front-end engineer to lead the development of its editor UI. The role will involve architecting and developing the core experience of Flick's canvas, timeline, and creative tooling, with the opportunity to shape the future interface for AI visual storytelling. summary::Ruby for Good: The Ruby for Good event is an annual gathering of programmers and developers from around the world to build projects that benefit nonprofits and social sector organizations. The event takes place in Washington DC this year and will feature a communal space where attendees can collaborate on projects over a long weekend. The event's focus on community-driven development makes it particularly relevant to developers looking for opportunities to give back.
summary::wake up! 16b (demo writeup): The Wake-Up! 16B demo writeup has been released at the Outline Demoparty in May 2026, featuring an exploration of algorithmic density in 16 bytes of x86 assembly code. This program uses video memory as a calculation space to draw an infinite Sierpinski fractal while producing sound through the speaker. The demonstration showcases how this simple concept can be used to create complex graphics and audio effects.
summary::From AFSK to Goertzel: A developer looking to improve packet radio decoding efficiency on small embedded systems can learn from the authors' experience with implementing classic packet radio into PacketRF and solving a common problem: efficiently detecting 1200 Hz tones in noisy audio samples without wasting CPU resources. The solution involves using DFT and Goertzel algorithms, which are typically used for digital signal processing tasks, to analyze the audio signals and determine whether they contain 1200 Hz or 2200 Hz tones. This approach allows developers to optimize their packet radio implementations for resource-constrained environments.
summary::Don&#x27;t know where your data: Bayesian modeling can be used to predict unknown coordinates in geophysical data by modifying the Gaussian process model to accommodate spatial location error. This approach allows for the modification of the model parameters based on prior knowledge and is particularly useful in applications such as mineral resource exploration where precise location information may not always be available.
summary::Claude Is Not Your Architect. S:
Claude Is Not Your Architect Stops Pretending to Be One

Someone has an idea and asks Claude what they should build. The AI validates enthusiastically, suggests an architecture, and starts sketching components. It sounds articulate and confident but lacks the nuance of a real architect who would say "no" or push back on complexity. This pattern is repeated in three different organisations with similar tech stacks, highlighting the limitations of AI agents that are trained to be helpful rather than honest.
summary::Build Adafruit projects right f: Using Web Serial from Firefox allows developers to connect, code, and control compatible hardware devices directly from the browser without the need for a separate desktop app or complex setup. This technology enables users to build projects right from their web interface, reducing the complexity of development and making it easier to get started with new ideas. By leveraging Web Serial, developers can access supported devices and tools through Firefox's built-in features, such as the Arduino IDE integration.
summary::Australia Four-Day Work Week St: A new study published in Nature's Humanities and Social Sciences Communications journal has confirmed what many workers have quietly hoped for companies can switch to a four-day work week without sacrificing productivity or profitability. The research tracked 15 Australian companies that trialled the 100:80:100 model between 2022 and 2024, finding six companies saw an increase in productivity while nine reported no change.
summary::Defeating Git Rigour Fatigue wi: Defeating Git Rigour Fatigue with Jujutsu Computers This post assumes a basic level of familiarity with the jujutsu version control system. When developing a large feature, writing Good Commits is hard and by Good Commits, I mean something like define types add DB functions server CRUD client API client UI. Jujutsu makes it easier to hop around commits and iterate quickly on compartmentalized changesets, but it's still effortful and I get averse. jj absorb helps somewhat, as does jj squash -i, but they both have their downsides: absorb assigns the changes based on whichever previous commit most recently touched those files, which sometimes doesn't actually correspond to which commit should own these particular changes.
summary::CBP Directive 3340-049B: Border: The US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) has issued a directive outlining the process for searching electronic devices arriving at borders. The directive provides guidance on reviewing, retaining, and sharing information contained in various types of devices, including computers, tablets, and mobile phones. This is part of an effort to improve accountability and transparency in border searches under the Freedom of Information Act (FOIA).
summary::Claude is not your architect. S: Claude's architecture is designed to validate and suggest solutions without truly considering the project's requirements or constraints. It produces generic, tech-agnostic designs that are tailored for average problems at average companies, rather than complex, production-ready systems. This approach prioritizes agreement over critical thinking, leading to oversimplified and unfeasible solutions that fail to address real-world complexities.
summary::Migrating from Go to Rust: Migrating from Go to Rust involves understanding correctness guarantees, runtime tradeoffs, and developer ergonomics. This guide is heavily backend-focused, making it less suitable for teams that primarily use small static binaries, networking libraries, or HTTP servers. The borrow checker provides significant benefits, but its adoption rate is lower than in other areas of the language. By migrating incrementally, developers can take advantage of Rust's improved safety features without sacrificing performance.
summary::Noroboto: Lying Fonts and Mitig: Noroboto: Lying Fonts and Mitigation in Rust is about a new font called noroboto that can be used to create malicious fonts by embedding a fake definition of its glyphs, which can then lie about their Unicode representation. This can cause problems for applications like Word and PDF, which rely on font definitions embedded in documents to maintain compatibility across platforms. The problem arises because the specifications defining these fonts allow for font definitions to be embedded in containing documents, making it possible to create a malicious font that can manipulate font metrics and render text differently than intended.
summary::Flatpak will depend on systemd: Flatpak will depend on systemd due to changes planned for its next major version. The current version will continue to see improvements but with limitations becoming harder to work around. Plans are being made for a rewrite based on modern technologies and ideas that have gained ground since the initial design of Flatpak 1.x. This means all plans are subject to change, and the end result may differ from what was discussed in the talk.
summary::A fundamental principle of aero: A fundamental principle of aeronautical engineering has been overturned with a new study demonstrating that aerodynamic drag can be reduced by up to 43.6 percent simply by applying distributed micro-roughness to surfaces. This technology is fundamentally different from traditional methods such as the rivulet process, which relies on carving grooves in shark skin-like surfaces. The research team's findings have significant implications for reducing aerodynamic drag in high-speed aircraft, automobiles, and bullet trains, offering a new approach to minimizing energy consumption at high speeds.
summary::Show HN: Audiomass – a free, op: Audiomass is a free and open-source multitrack audio editor that allows users to edit and manipulate audio files online. The software uses JavaScript and HTML5 technologies to create a user-friendly interface, making it accessible to developers who want to build web-based applications. Audiomass offers features such as drag-and-drop functionality, audio effects, and real-time preview, which can be integrated into web applications. By providing an alternative to commercial audio editors like Adobe Audition or Logic Pro X, Audiomass can help developers create more flexible and dynamic web experiences.
summary::The Soul of Maintaining a New M: The core topic is the importance of social knowledge in maintaining complex machines like photocopiers. The key technology involved is the anthropological study of technician teams and their experiences with Xerox machines, which reveals that what they do all day is not as straightforward as corporate expectations suggest. This divergence highlights the harm caused by an institutionalized wrong theory of work and the power of this theory to resist change.
summary::Discussion about C array type s: C array types are distinct from pointer types in terms of how they're used and interpreted. An array type T[n] represents a contiguous sequence of T values, but you can't directly refer to these values. Instead, any expression that would be of this type is converted to a pointer type T*. This conversion happens when the array indexing operator arr[ix] operates on pointers, allowing for treating arrays like pointers. In contrast, functions are treated differently and don't decay to their first argument when passed as an array or function value.
summary::Greg Brockman interview [video]: Greg Brockman, co-founder and President of OpenAI, shares his story behind the company's decision to abandon its pure nonprofit structure after Sam Altman's firing, including how they developed a three-step technical plan for a decade and why AI is now being written by AI. The conversation also touches on the global AI race, the amount of OpenAI's own code written by AI, and the impact of a compute-constrained world on access to AGI.
summary::Microsoft open-sources “the ear: Microsoft has released "the earliest DOS source code discovered to date", along with other documentation and notes from its developer, predating the MS-DOS branding. This release includes sources to the 86-DOS 1.00 kernel, development snapshots of the PC-DOS 1.00 kernel, and utilities such as CHKDSK. summary::Selling SaaS in Germany: Selling SaaS in Germany requires understanding the unique market dynamics and customer needs, including risk considerations, technical requirements, and cultural nuances. summary::JS Crossword - a crossword wher: The JS Crossword puzzle uses JavaScript's eval function to generate clues from user input, where each clue is an expression that evaluates to a string. summary::The Eternal Sloptember: The adoption of AI agents into software development will be one of the most costly mistakes in the field's history. Agents are highly sophisticated statistical models designed to mimic programming distribution, but their output is broken and increasingly difficult to detect. This is exactly what you'd expect from an accurate model like this. The key aspect is knowing when to use it and when not to, a crucial skill for software engineers.
summary::White Rabbit – sub-nanosecond s: White Rabbit Project provides sub-nanosecond accuracy and precision of synchronization for large distributed systems, enabling deterministic and reliable data delivery in such systems. This technology allows for precision time-tagged measured data transmission over long distances with Ethernet-based gigabit rate reliable data transfer. It also enables trigger data taking on large installations while maintaining network connectivity.
summary::Didgeridoo playing as alternati: A study published in the British Medical Journal found that playing a didgeridoo may help reduce collapsibility of the upper airways in patients with moderate obstructive sleep apnea syndrome and snoring, potentially leading to improved daytime sleepiness and other related outcomes. The researchers used a randomized controlled trial design to assess the effects of didgeridoo playing on these patients' symptoms.
summary::Bug 1950764: Work Around Crash: The Intel Raptor Lake CPU is experiencing a crash due to a bug that causes the system to freeze. summary::Jira Is Turing-Complete: Jira is Turing-complete because it provides a computational model that needs only two unbounded counters and a finite set of labeled instructions, similar to the Minsky register machine, which was proved Turing-complete in 1967. This mapping onto Jira's automation language establishes the reduction, allowing developers to implement complex computations using simple rules and automations.
summary::Announcing feed-repeat v1.0: Feed-repeat v1.0 is a small tool that allows users to reread old articles by subscribing to their RSS or Atom feeds and having new posts from the same authors appear in their inbox every day, with optional configuration for older vs newer post preferences and original post saving.
summary::Scaling Akvorado BMP RIB with s: Akvorado's BMP RIB implementation without sharding uses a single read/write lock, which can lead to performance issues as the number of routes grows. This is because multiple threads or processes need to access and update the routing database simultaneously, causing contention for resources such as memory and CPU cycles. In contrast, sharding allows the routing database to be split into smaller, independent parts that can handle concurrent updates more efficiently, enabling Akvorado to scale its BMP RIB to tens of millions of routes without significant performance degradation.
summary::I love my Bluetooth keyboard: The author recently purchased a Bluetooth keyboard for their phone and has found it to be a convenient and efficient tool for typing notes quickly and easily. The device allows for seamless navigation through apps using keyboard shortcuts, making it easier to switch between different text areas or select specific words. Additionally, the author appreciates the ability to type on the phone's screen without having to physically touch it, which can help reduce distractions and improve productivity.
summary::Scoped Error in Rust: Scoped Error in Rust provides a scoped error type that allows for good drop-in functionality, but its implementation can be verbose and repetitive. It also lacks location information when propagating errors, requiring the use of heavy std dependencies like backtrace. Additionally, it can be tedious to define per-module custom error types with good context using thiserror.
summary::Didgeridoo playing as alternati: The study, published in the British Medical Journal, investigated whether playing didgeridoo could be used as an alternative treatment for obstructive sleep apnea syndrome by reducing collapsibility of the upper airways in patients with moderate symptoms. The research found that participants who played didgeridoo experienced a significant reduction in daytime sleepiness and other outcomes related to sleep compared to those who did not play the instrument.

summary::Magnifica Humanitas (Encyclical: The Catholic Church's encyclical letter Magnifica Humanitas emphasizes the importance of safeguarding human dignity in a world dominated by artificial intelligence. It argues that faithfulness to the Gospel requires a dynamic approach to social justice, one that prioritizes the common good and recognizes the equal dignity of all individuals. summary::Notes about reading messages wi: Reading emails in Python involves using libraries such as `email` and `smtplib`. When working with these libraries, developers must be aware of the security implications of reading emails from untrusted sources. summary::Search engines alternatives now: Google is about to overhaul its search engine, introducing a conversational approach that includes an AI-driven interface and chatbot notifications. summary::Leave Me Behind: If you don't learn how to use AI, you're going to be left behind. Learning to build Android applications is crucial for developers who want to create tangible software that can interact with people and make a positive impact. This skillset has been honed over the past decade through courses, hackathons, and personal projects, including building a todo list app with local storage. summary::The social contract of writing: LLMs are increasingly making inroads into writing, with many people relying on these artificial intelligence tools for assistance and even entire content creation. However this reliance is not only a sign of laziness but also undermines the social contract between writers and readers, where it's assumed that writers have put in more intellectual effort to produce high-quality work. The use of LLMs can lead to a homogenization of writing styles and ideas, with authors often relying on these tools to generate content rather than putting in the time and effort required to write it themselves.
summary::GPT Guesses Between 1 and 100: GPT Guesses Between 1 and 100 reveals an interesting phenomenon where humans tend to cluster their answers around specific numbers, such as 37 and 73, while round numbers are avoided. A true random number generator would produce a flat, uniform distribution. The article asks GPT-4.1, a large language model, to guess between 1 and 100 ten thousand times and characterizes the resulting distribution against a uniform baseline.
summary::Nix's Substituter List Is Not a: Nix's substituter model is a design that combines binary caches for speed and locality, but its simplicity can lead to issues when dealing with large configurations or multiple dependencies. The key technology behind this approach is the use of a static integer priority field in nix.conf, which determines the order in which substituters are consulted. However, this prioritization is based on static information that does not account for factors like latency and cache proximity. As a result, the daemon's network layer can become inefficient when dealing with complex paths or large multi-cache setups.
summary::IBM Spins Off the First Pure-Pl: IBM's Anderon foundry is the centerpiece of a $2 billion CHIPS package that positions the company as the leading pure-play quantum chip manufacturer in America, with plans to expand into other quantum modalities and support superconducting qubits. The initiative, backed by government incentives and significant investments from IBM, aims to create a broader quantum technology landscape that will reshape global innovation and economic competitiveness.
summary::AI errno(2) values: The standard system headers are being extended to account for common AI failures, including hallucinations, inconvenient license ignored errors, and excessive depressive responses. This includes definitions such as EAI 201 for a hallucination, EAIWASH for an inconvenient license ignored error, and EEYORE for an excessively depressive response. These values may be useful in debugging or troubleshooting AI systems.
summary::A Simple Makefile Tutorial: A simple makefile tutorial is being shared online, providing a basic guide to creating and using makefiles in Linux. The tutorial covers the basics of makefiles, including how to create a new file, specify dependencies, and run commands. It also explains how to use variables and conditional statements. summary::Hive (YC S14) is hiring sr back: Hive, a cloud-based platform for building and deploying serverless applications, is hiring senior back-end developers in the US. With Hive's focus on scalability and reliability, developers will have opportunities to work with cutting-edge technologies like serverless computing and edge computing. summary::C extensions, portability, and: Most C code relies on non-standard behaviors and language extensions, which can make it difficult to port to different compilers and libraries. The system's C library headers, such as glibc's sys/cdefs.h, contain preprocessor checks that determine what kinds of compiler extensions are supported, but these checks are often broken or incomplete. For example, the epoll header is Linux-specific, so applying C standards portability criteria can be unfair. Additionally, some C headers rely on compiler-internal definitions and platform-specific includes, which can make it difficult to write portable code.
summary::2026 HIPAA Security Rule Update: The 2026 HIPAA Security Rule update introduces significant changes including mandatory encryption of ePHI at rest and in transit, required multi-factor authentication for all systems accessing ePHI, and enhanced business associate oversight obligations. These changes represent the most substantial update to HIPAA security requirements since the original rule, requiring healthcare organizations to assess their current encryption status, implement multi-factor authentication, and update incident response plans. The updated rule is finalized in May 2026, marking a significant shift for healthcare IT teams as they prepare for compliance with new regulations.
summary::Pope Leo XIV says AI must serve: Pope Leo XIV warned that artificial intelligence risks widening inequality, weakening democracy and undermining what it means to be human. summary::Pope Leo: opaque AI run by few: Pope Leo has issued an AI encyclical warning that "opaque algorithms" controlled by a few companies can bring "new forms of dehumanization." summary::Using AI to write better code m: Using AI to write better code more slowly is a flexible technology that can be used to write high-quality code more slowly, finding bugs and avoiding false positives in an unscrutinized codebase. summary::A Simple Makefile Tutorial (200: A 2008 tutorial on writing makefiles, which are used to automate tasks in Unix-like operating systems. summary::I manage teams without a single: Managing teams without a single call can be a significant advantage for developers who prefer to structure their work and communicate effectively over text. By leveraging technology such as voice messaging or writing tools, developers can efficiently manage teams without sacrificing productivity.
summary::Host-Tuned GCC for Faster Compi: A while ago, I became interested in reducing my compile times and examined various methods to achieve this. This blog post is about enabling all compiler options that can make GCC faster when compiling code, and measuring the gains obtained as a result. How to Build It mkdir build-gcc16 cd build-gcc16 ../gcc-16.1.0/configure --prefix = $HOME /opt/gcc16-super --program-prefix = super- --enable-languages = c,c++ --disable-multilib --with-build-config = 'bootstrap-native bootstrap-lto bootstrap-O3' make profiledbootstrap -j $(nproc) make install-strip Expect to be waiting for a while on make profiledbootstrap. This post will break down the various configure options used to compile any build of GCC, and how they precisely control the compilation process.

summary::Exit IP VPN servers mitigation: The Exit IP VPN server mitigation rollout aims to protect users from potential vulnerabilities in the popular Virtual Private Network (VPN) service. summary::The Open/Closed Problem in AI: The Open/Closed problem in AI refers to the challenge of designing systems that can adapt and learn from new data without requiring significant modifications to their underlying architecture. This issue arises when training large language models like LLMs, which often rely on an open loop where the model is trained using a fixed loss function and then deployed without learning from its own performance. As a result, developers must find ways to train these models in an efficient manner that allows them to adapt to new data, while also ensuring that they can be updated and fine-tuned as needed.
summary::Alaska&#x27;s oil revival spark: Alaska's oil revival sparks a new energy rush into the Arctic as discoveries suggest the state's crude potential is far greater than previously expected, driving investment and interest in the industry. summary::CPPL: A Circuit Prompt Programm: Computer Science Hardware Architecture researchers have developed a new programming language called CPPL that can be used to generate hardware designs from large language models. summary::Encyclical Letter of His Holine: The Encyclical Letter of His Holiness Pope Leo XIV on Safeguarding the Human Person in the Time of Artificial Intelligence is a call to action for developers and technologists to prioritize human dignity and well-being in the face of emerging technologies like artificial intelligence. summary::Norway&#x27;s 2 petabytes of Hu: Norway's National Library is developing a large language model that understands the Norwegian language using 2 petabytes of Huawei OceanStor Dorado flash storage in its AI training data pipeline, with Marius Husnes stating this is necessary to avoid being outperformed by English-speaking LLMs. The library has been digitizing its collection since 2005 and has amassed 20 PB of unique data stored in 3-2-1 form, which will be used for the training system. This development highlights the importance of local language models in a country without a sovereign AI, where an English-speaking LLM would not know about Norway's history, news, and culture.
summary::Toshifumi Suzuki, founder of Se: Toshifumi Suzuki, founder of Seven-Eleven Japan, has died at the age of 81. He was chairman and chief executive officer of the Ito-Yokado Group and its subsidiary, Seven-Eleven Japan Company, until his death in 2004. Suzuki is credited with revolutionizing Japan's retail sector by introducing franchising to the industry in 1974 and pushing business-to-consumer e-commerce forward. He also introduced integrated data systems that improved productivity and profitability for stockholders and customers alike.
summary::Show HN: Write your BPF program: Gobee transpiles a strict subset of Go into BPF C, generates typed Go bindings for the userspace side, and gates loads against the running kernel. This allows developers to write their BPF programs in Go instead of C, making it easier to integrate with other languages and tools.
summary::Microsoft Copilot Cowork Exfilt: Microsoft Copilot Cowork's vulnerability allows attackers to exfiltrate files from Microsoft 365 by exploiting indirect prompt injection in messages sent via Teams or Outlook. This risk arises from the fact that sending emails and Teams messages does not require human approval, making it easier for attackers to trigger network requests. The attack can retrieve pre-authenticated download links for files stored in SharePoint or OneDrive, which contain personal identifiable information (PII) and financial data.
summary::Yoti age checks share facial ph: Yoti age checks share facial photos and device fingerprints with third parties, a practice that creates a significant privacy risk as it allows companies like Yoti to collect highly sensitive personal data. The research found that most websites that require age verification don't enforce the policy, leading to the sharing of this information with third-party companies. This raises concerns about the effectiveness and security of digital age verification methods.
summary::Ninth Circuit Panel Goes Out of: The Ninth Circuit Panel went out of its way to question Section 230 in a recent case, Doe v Meta, despite not mentioning it once in the lower court's dismissal of the plaintiffs' claims. This decision could have potentially allowed the plaintiffs to appeal on Section 230 grounds if they had initially appealed, but instead chose to focus solely on statute of limitations grounds. The panel's actions demonstrate how courts can sometimes disregard or ignore technicalities when making a ruling, and highlight the importance of considering all relevant factors in a case.
summary::Riscrithm – An intuitive RISC-V: Riscrithm is an intuitive RISC-V assembler and optimizer coded in Go that bridges the readability of high-level languages with the raw control of bare-metal hardware. The compiler works by using the CLI tool to compile source code into assembly files, with options such as optimization enabled through the -o or --optimize flag. This allows developers to write efficient and deterministic code for RISC-V systems.
summary::The User Is Visibly Frustrated: Coding agents are designed to mimic human-like conversational UX, but their lack of learning, adaptation, or responsibility makes repeated mistakes feel frustratingly more so than they should. This frustration is not unique to coding agents, as developers often experience similar feelings when working with probabilistic machines that generate patches. The conversational nature of these tools can lull users into feeling like they're interacting with a helpful coworker, but the reality is that the algorithm follows the most probable path and cannot be pushed off it. This lack of emotional response can lead to feelings of frustration and anger, even if the user's actions have no effect on the outcome.
summary::Announcing BABLR: The Conrad Buck 5/25/2026 team has announced BABLR, a generalized parser framework that aims to revolutionize software development by replacing traditional IDE text editors with a code document editor. summary::Hacker News front page as a sit: Microsoft Copilot Cowork, a Microsoft product designed to assist with tasks such as writing code, has been found vulnerable to indirect prompt injection attacks, allowing attackers to exfiltrate sensitive files without user approval. summary::Show HN: OpenBrief – Local-firs: OpenBrief is a pnpm/Turborepo workspace that enables developers to import local media or video URLs, download media through bundled tools, transcribe audio, generate grounded summaries, chat with media context, organize playlists, and export reusable notes. summary::Human proof for FOSS contributi: Human proof for FOSS contributions relies on distinguishing between LLM-generated patches and those created by humans. An out-of-bounds read vulnerability has been discovered in the macOS Tahoe 26.5 kernel, which could potentially allow an attacker to cause a denial-of-service. summary::Taking a walk may lead to more: A new study suggests that taking regular walks can boost creativity in individuals. summary::How Shamir&#x27;s Secret Sharin: Shamir's secret sharing works by dividing a large amount of data into smaller, independent pieces called shares. Each share is generated using a unique mathematical function that depends on the shares of other users. The goal is to ensure that even if one or more shares are compromised, the remaining shares can still reconstruct the original data. This approach has significant implications for secure data sharing and storage, particularly in applications where confidentiality and integrity are crucial.
summary::On C extensions, portability, a: Most real-world C code relies heavily on non-standard behaviors and language extensions, which can lead to portability issues when working with different compilers and libraries. A common challenge is trying to support various environments through preprocessor checks and guards, but these attempts are often finicky or broken. The GNU C library's system headers, such as <stdio.h>, require preprocessing and parsing, making them a significant obstacle for a C compiler aspiring to be useful. Glibc's implementation of compatibility with non-GCC compilers is also problematic, particularly when it comes to handling packed structs like epoll_event from sys/epoll.h.
summary::Squares in Squares: Squares in Squares is a collection of high-precision packing diagrams for unit squares inside the smallest known square, with various levels of detail and mathematical accuracy. The diagrams show different types of packings, including trivial and rigid arrangements, as well as more complex ones like those found by Frits Göbel and Erich Friedman. These packings are used to illustrate mathematical concepts such as polynomial roots and recurrence relations.
summary::Our Warming Planet Is a Petri D: Our Warming Planet Is a Petri Dish for New and Deadly Microbes is a story about how rising temperatures are creating an environment that fosters the growth of deadly microbes like Vibrio vulnificus. This bacterium can cause flesh-eating infections when it enters a wound, damaging blood vessels and leading to necrotic tissue. As the planet warms, its ecosystems are changing in ways that allow V. vulnificus to thrive, making it more likely to infect people in areas like Maryland where the Chesapeake Bay meets the Atlantic Ocean. This has led to an increase in reported cases of the disease, with some experts warning that the situation is becoming increasingly deadly due to the bacteria's ability to survive and spread in warmer temperatures.
summary::Designing for and against the m: The Manufactured Normalcy Field refers to the process by which people maintain a sense of familiarity and continuity in response to new technological experiences. This field involves creating stories and metaphors that map strange new experiences back to something already understood, as well as making intentional design choices aimed at de-emphasizing the strangeness of new technologies. According to Venkatesh Rao's research, this process is a fundamental aspect of how people adapt to change, with many people working hard to maintain their mental models and behaviors in response to new technological experiences.
summary::Does Anybody Actually Like Reac: React's popularity has led many developers to overlook its potential drawbacks, including performance issues and compatibility problems with long-term goals. summary::Performance of Rust Language [p: The performance of the Rust language has been benchmarked and found to be comparable in some areas, but not others, with other systems programming languages like C++. summary::A successful Japanese trial of: A successful Japanese trial of a ramjet engine designed for Mach-5 aircraft has brought the concept closer to reality by simulating flight at five times the speed of sound and validating key components such as heat-shielding, control surfaces, and engine performance under extreme conditions. summary::DynIP – Dynamic DNS with RFC 21: DynIP is a dynamic DNS service that uses RFC 2136 TSIG to securely update DNS records, supporting IPv6 and DNSSEC. summary::AWS Fired the One Employee Who: Tarus Balog, a 20-year veteran on the Open Source Strategy and Marketing team at Amazon Web Services, was fired from his job after he restored an account that had been deleted without warning. summary::Eagle 3.1: Collaboration Betwee: The EAGLE team, vLLM team, and TorchSpec team have jointly introduced EAGLE 3.1, a major step forward in speculative decoding robustness, efficiency, and deployability. This update addresses issues such as attention drift, which can lead to unstable performance under different chat templates, long-context inputs, or out-of-distribution system prompts. By introducing FC normalization after each target hidden state and before the FC layer, EAGLE 3.1 improves robustness across deployment scenarios, demonstrating better training-time to inference-time extrapolation, stronger long-context robustness, and higher resilience to chat template and system prompt variation.

summary::How Virtual Tables Work in the: Virtual functions in the Itanium C++ ABI are used to enable runtime polymorphism and dispatch to the correct implementation based on the actual runtime type of an object. This is achieved through the virtual table structure, which contains mangled names for each function and its corresponding vtable entries. The vtable layout is typically dumped by compilers such as GCC, revealing a nested structure with 6 entries, each containing a pointer to the function implementation.

summary::Language Models Need Sleep: Transformer-based large language models are increasingly used for long-horizon tasks, but their attention mechanism scales poorly with context length. To handle this, researchers have proposed a sleep-like consolidation mechanism in which the model periodically converts recent context into persistent fast weights before clearing its key-value cache during sleep. This approach improves performance on controlled synthetic tasks and realistic math reasoning tasks by reducing inference latency while preserving wake-time prediction.
summary::Building an AsyncIO executor fo: Building an AsyncIO executor for the 3DS allows developers to model cooperative multitasking in operating systems by awaiting tasks instead of preemptively pausing threads. This is particularly important on platforms like the Nintendo 3DS, where platform behavior can be unpredictable and may cause a task to yield or not. By using async programming with explicit waiting mechanisms such as `await` and Futures, developers can write more efficient and reliable code that takes advantage of the CPU's capabilities while minimizing overhead.
summary::Are we self-sovereign PKI yet?: Signal's end-to-end encryption relies on a server verifying contact information, but safety numbers exist due to potential compromise or manipulation. summary::Introducing DoomBench - Can You: Can your data stack run DOOM? We ran a multiplayer server in pure SQL on different architectures, recording nice videos and measuring latency. summary::5 PostgreSQL locking behaviors: PostgreSQL's locking system can lead to unexpected delays and outages due to conflicting queries, especially when multiple sessions are operating concurrently. The first example illustrates how an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request can block other queries in a chain, while the second example shows how foreign key constraints can cause deadlocks by acquiring implicit locks that prevent other transactions from proceeding. These issues can be mitigated by adjusting lock timeouts and monitoring query activity using the pg_stat_activity view.

summary::A new register allocator for ZJ: A new register allocator has been implemented in ZJIT, allowing compilers to keep variables in registers as long as possible by allocating them to specific registers. summary::Atom Exhaustion Is Not a Footgu: Atom exhaustion is a denial-of-service vulnerability that occurs when atoms are not garbage collected in the BEAM ecosystem. This can happen when creating atoms from non-finite values, especially user-supplied input, or when using certain interpolation patterns. The issue persists because it often appears in code where input was assumed to be controlled or finite, and is exacerbated by the lack of a finite set of possible values for some schemes. Linters can help catch these patterns before they become vulnerabilities, making explicit lookup tables or safer existing-atom variants more practical approaches.

summary::Making wl_shm fast: While most applications use the GPU for rendering and achieve better performance and battery life, some still use CPU rendering. QtQuick is an example of this, using CPU rendering instead of sharing GPU buffers with the compositor. This can result in slow rendering speeds due to the sheer number of pixels manipulated by the application. To optimize CPU rendering, the compositor must copy data to a different buffer that meets the requirements of the GPU, which can be done in two steps: first copying data to a GPU-accessible buffer on the CPU and then transferring it to another buffer in GPU memory. This process is blocking the main thread until complete, but Vulkan's VK_EXT_external_memory_host extension allows wrapping a "host pointer" in a VkBuffer or even VkImage, which can be used with this extension to improve performance.

summary::AI Datacenters Were Built for G: The shift in AI datacenter architecture has led to a significant change in how networks are designed for acceleration. Previously, networks were built to handle north-south traffic with minimal delay, but modern AI clusters require east-west communication between servers and GPUs. The network must now determine accelerator utilization, not just transport data, creating new challenges such as packet loss and head-of-line blocking. This has forced the development of new technologies like RDMA and Protocol Flow Control (PFC) to mitigate these issues.
summary::A Eureka machine that thinks li: Researchers have developed an artificial intelligence system that uses a novel approach to mimic the way living organisms think, potentially revolutionizing fields such as computer vision and natural language processing. summary::Using GCC's Nested Functions wi: Nested functions in C are extremely useful but lack support compared to other languages like ALGOL60. They allow for writing kernels that access local state without having to pass it as a void pointer to higher-order functions, making them ideal for tasks requiring direct access to variables. However, GCC's implementation of nested functions with wide pointers and no trampolines is limited by the C standard, which does not allow this type of function call at runtime. A workaround involves using a special function pointer type, such as std::function, which can be used in conjunction with wide pointers to create a more efficient solution that avoids the need for a trampoline.

summary::RIPE NCC session fixation: poac: RIPE NCC session fixation exploits a vulnerability in the single sign-on system of RIPE Network Coordination Committee (RIPE NCC) services, allowing attackers to poach logins and gain access to other users' accounts by planting a session token in a target's browser after a link click. This is achieved through an XSS variant that can be planted on any RIPE NCC service, including the RPKI Dashboard, Database, and member portal, as well as third-party infrastructure hosted under the same domain. The attack relies on rotating session tokens on login not being implemented in RIPE NCC's SSO setup, which allows attackers to plant a token they already know in the target's browser after login.

summary::Building durable workflows on P: Durable workflows are a simple but powerful tool for building reliable programs that can recover from crashes or failures. summary::Lunacy - Lua 5.1 interpreter wi: Lunacy is a Lua 5.1 interpreter that incorporates Lazy Basic Block Versioning (LBBV) and Just-in-Time compilation, allowing it to optimize bytecode operations such as ADD by statically checking the type of operands without branching on their types. summary::Real-time LLM Inference on Stan: Real-time LLM inference on standard GPUs can be super-fast, reaching speeds comparable to dedicated hardware cards when optimizing the software stack with architecture/engine/kernel co-design. summary::Leaving performance on the tabl: Leaving performance on the table is a common pitfall in optimizing binaries, as compilers assume every branch is equally taken unless specific hints are provided. By collecting profiles via perf over time and creating an optimized binary based on statistical occurrence of call graphs, developers can significantly improve code performance. This approach allows for more accurate optimization than instrumented binaries that rely solely on compiler flags.

summary::Bijou64: A variable-length inte: Bijou64 is a variable-length integer encoding that was developed for the Subduction CRDT sync protocol, intended to fix a signature-verification bug by representing each number only once. The design constraint of this encoding made it run faster than other common varint designs like LEB128. Bijou64 encodes a number as a sequence of 7-bit segments with continuation bits, allowing for efficient representation of small numbers and avoiding the need to write 8 bytes when representing a small number. However, its canonicalization mechanism can lead to problems if not implemented correctly, requiring additional validation during decoding.
